meta.name: Read the Room — Question Types Pack (expanded)
meta.purpose: Adds new question TYPES and content FLAVORS to the PI Training quiz alongside the existing multiple-choice questions. Merge into read-the-room-question-bank.json (same top-level 'questions' array) or load as an additional bank. The app selects behavior by the 'type' field. Existing questions with no 'type' should be treated as 'mc'.
meta.source: Original items built on Predictive Index concepts (People Data Toolkit). PI-accurate.

mc.description: Multiple choice, 4 options, one correct. The existing type. The 'spot the drive', 'odd one out', 'best move', and 'spot the false' items below are ALL just mc — no new code needed, only richer content. They may carry a 'style' tag for flavor/UI hints but render as normal mc.
mc.fields: id, type:'mc', tier, topic, q, options[4], answer (index 0-3), teach, [optional style]

tf.description: True/false. Two big tiles: TRUE (green) / FALSE (red) on host and phone. Scored like mc (speed + correctness, streak bonus).
tf.fields: id, type:'tf', tier, topic, q (a statement), answer (true|false), teach
tf.antiCheat: Shuffle which side TRUE/FALSE appears on, per question, host and phone in sync (same principle as the mc position shuffle).

slider.description: Interactive 'build the graph': drag one or more factor sliders on a Low↔High (0-100) scale to place a profile. Scored by CLOSENESS to a target, not right/wrong.
slider.fields: id, type:'slider', tier, topic, q, factors:[{key:A|B|C|D, label, target 0-100, low, high}], tolerance, teach
slider.scoring: perFactor = max(0, 1 - abs(placed - target)/scaleDenominator) with full credit inside `tolerance`; average across factors; then apply the usual speed bonus. Reveal shows target vs. placed + the teach line.

graph.description: Read the Pattern: the host renders an actual PI pattern (dots/line across the four Low↔High continua) from the given values, then asks an mc question about it. Scored like mc.
graph.fields: id, type:'graph', tier, topic, q, pattern:{A,B,C,D each 0-100}, options[4], answer (index 0-3), teach
graph.render: Host shows the four continua (Collaborative↔Independent, Reserved↔Sociable, Driving↔Steady, Flexible↔Precise) with a dot on each at the given 0-100 value, plus the mc tiles. Phone shows the four shape tiles (in-person) or the pattern too (online mode). Same position-shuffle + scoring as mc.

order.description: Put it in order: the player drags 3-4 items into the correct sequence on their phone. Scored by closeness to the correct order.
order.fields: id, type:'order', tier, topic, q (states the direction, e.g. 'lowest to highest'), items:[array IN CORRECT ORDER], teach
order.scoring: Present items shuffled. Score = fraction of items in their correct position (or correct adjacent pairs) — full points if the whole order is right, partial otherwise; then apply the speed bonus. Reveal shows the correct order + teach line.
order.note: The app must store the player's submitted order; this likely needs a new answer field the current Firebase rules don't allow — add it to database.rules.json (validated, scoped under rooms/{pin}) and have the user publish.

questions.q: Factor A (Dominance) is the drive to exert influence on people and events.
questions.teach: True. Dominance = the drive to take charge and shape outcomes.

questions.q: A person high in Extraversion tends to be reserved and prefers working alone.
questions.teach: False. High Extraversion is outgoing and social — reserved is LOW B.

questions.q: High Patience means someone prefers a steady, consistent pace over constant change.
questions.teach: True. High C craves stability and a calm, steady pace.

questions.q: Someone low in Formality is careful, precise, and loves detailed rules.
questions.teach: False. Low Formality is flexible and casual — precise is HIGH D.

questions.q: A low-Dominance person is generally cooperative and harmony-seeking.
questions.teach: True. Low A is collaborative and avoids conflict.

questions.q: A high-Extraversion person is motivated by public recognition and social connection.
questions.teach: True. High B wants interaction, visibility, and social acceptance.

questions.q: Low Patience people are usually calm, unhurried, and slow to act.
questions.teach: False. Low C is driving, fast-paced, and impatient — calm is HIGH C.

questions.q: High Formality people want clear expectations, rules, and certainty.
questions.teach: True. High D needs structure, specifics, and freedom from error.

questions.q: A low-Extraversion person is energized by constant meetings and being the center of attention.
questions.teach: False. Low B wants privacy, reflection time, and to work with facts.

questions.q: High Dominance shows up as assertive, independent, and comfortable with conflict.
questions.teach: True. Those are classic high-A behaviors.

questions.q: When Dominance is higher than Extraversion (A > B), the person is usually more task-oriented than people-oriented.
questions.teach: True. A > B = task-oriented: problem solver, holds authority tightly.

questions.q: A B > A person tends to delegate easily and work collaboratively.
questions.teach: True. B > A = people-oriented: empathetic, delegates, team-comfortable.

questions.q: Someone with A > C is patient, steady, and slow to take initiative.
questions.teach: False. A > C = proactive: takes initiative, fast-paced, impatient with routine.

questions.q: A > D (Dominance over Formality) signals comfort with risk and quick decisions.
questions.teach: True. A > D = comfortable with risk: independent, decisive, venturesome.

questions.q: A D > A person is a bold risk-taker who ignores rules.
questions.teach: False. D > A = cautious with risk: careful, conservative, needs clear rules.

questions.q: B > C describes someone who connects quickly and is enthusiastic and fast-talking.
questions.teach: True. B > C = quick to connect: lively, persuasive, builds relationships fast.

questions.q: A C > B person tends to think before speaking and take time to open up.
questions.teach: True. C > B = takes time to connect: reserved, organizes thoughts first.

questions.q: D > C people treat deadlines as flexible and rules as loose guidelines.
questions.teach: False. D > C = careful with rules: precise, strict on correctness and deadlines.

questions.q: The wider the gap between two factors, the stronger that relationship shows up in behavior.
questions.teach: True. A wider spread (especially across the midpoint) = stronger expression.

questions.q: The Maverick is a cautious, detail-focused rule-follower.
questions.teach: False. The Maverick is a bold, risk-taking, outside-the-box innovator.

questions.q: A Collaborator is warm, patient, a good team player, and tends to dislike risk.
questions.teach: True. That's the Collaborator profile.

questions.q: A Specialist prefers ambiguity and dislikes structure and rules.
questions.teach: False. The Specialist needs strong structure, precision, and respects authority.

questions.q: Factor E measures whether a person tends to decide subjectively (gut) or objectively (facts/logic).
questions.teach: True. Factor E: subjective vs. objective at the point of decision.

questions.q: The Self pattern reflects how a person thinks they must adapt to their current job.
questions.teach: False. That's Self-Concept. Self = a person's natural, basic drives.

questions.q: The Synthesis pattern is the average of Self and Self-Concept — how someone usually behaves at work.
questions.teach: True. Synthesis = Self + Self-Concept, the everyday work behavior.

questions.q: Factor M is the person's cognitive ability score.
questions.teach: False. Factor M = how many words were selected. Cognitive ability is the separate CA.

questions.q: A good readback uses plain language and describes the person, not PI jargon.
questions.teach: True. Stay neutral, describe a person, lead with strengths, skip the jargon.

questions.q: There are right and wrong answers on the PI Behavioral Assessment.
questions.teach: False. The Behavioral Assessment has no right or wrong answers.

questions.q: The PI Cognitive Assessment is a 12-minute, 50-question test of general cognitive ability.
questions.teach: True. That's exactly what the CA is.

questions.q: As a factor moves farther from the midpoint, its behavior becomes weaker and less noticeable.
questions.teach: False. Farther from the midpoint = STRONGER, more forceful expression.

questions.q: A coworker says: "Just give me the bottom line — let's decide and move." Which drive is showing?
questions.teach: Take-charge and decisive = high Dominance (often with low Patience).

questions.q: "I need to double-check every detail before this goes out." Which drive is high?
questions.teach: Careful, detail-checking, does-it-right = high Formality.

questions.q: "Let's all grab coffee and talk it through together first." Which drive is high?
questions.teach: Seeking people and conversation = high Extraversion.

questions.q: "I'd rather keep using the proven process than switch things up again." Which drive is high?
questions.teach: Preferring stability and the familiar over change = high Patience.

questions.q: Which behavior does NOT fit a HIGH Dominance person?
questions.teach: Harmony-seeking is a LOW Dominance trait; the others are high A.

questions.q: Which does NOT fit HIGH Extraversion?
questions.teach: Introspective is a LOW Extraversion trait; the others are high B.

questions.q: Which does NOT fit HIGH Patience?
questions.teach: Restless is a LOW Patience trait; the others are high C.

questions.q: Which does NOT fit HIGH Formality?
questions.teach: Spontaneous is a LOW Formality trait; the others are high D.

questions.q: You must give tough feedback to a teammate who is high Formality and low Extraversion. Best move?
options: Raise it in the team meeting
A private, prepared one-on-one with a clear plan
Send a quick group chat note
questions.teach: Low B → not in public; high D → needs structure. Private + prepared fits both.

questions.q: A high-Dominance, low-Patience employee is bored and restless. Best move?
options: Give them a fast, high-ownership challenge
Tell them to slow down and follow routine
questions.teach: High A + low C needs an outlet — a fast, high-ownership challenge channels the drive.

questions.q: You're rolling out a big change to a high-Patience, high-Formality person. Best move?
options: Spring it on them and ask them to adapt
Give notice, a clear step-by-step plan, and time
Tell them not to worry about it
Make them announce it to everyone
questions.teach: High C needs time/warning; high D needs structure. Notice + a clear plan settles both.

questions.q: Which statement about Factor C (Patience) is FALSE?
options: High C likes a steady pace
Low C is impatient with routine
High C loves constant change and variety
questions.teach: False one: high C prefers stability — it's LOW C that wants variety and change.

questions.q: Which statement about the PI Cognitive Assessment is FALSE?
questions.teach: False one: the CA measures cognitive ability ('g'), not personality — that's the Behavioral Assessment.

questions.q: Read this pattern. Which Reference Profile is it?
questions.teach: High A, high B, low C, low D — bold, social, fast, freedom from rules = Maverick.

questions.q: Read this pattern. Which Reference Profile is it?
questions.teach: Low A/B, high C/D — precise, steady, structured, supportive = Guardian.

questions.q: Read this pattern. Which Reference Profile is it?
questions.teach: Very high B, low C/D — a complete extravert who sells and delegates = Promoter.

questions.q: Look at this pattern. Is this person HIGH or LOW in Formality (Factor D)?
questions.teach: Factor D sits far to the 'Precise' (high) end — clearly high Formality.

questions.q: Look at this pattern. Which single drive is the MOST extreme (farthest from the midpoint)?
questions.teach: Dominance is way out at ~92 while the others hug the midpoint — A is the strongest drive.

questions.q: Order these people from LOWEST to HIGHEST Dominance.
items: "Whatever the team decides is fine with me."
"I'll share my view but I'm flexible."
"I think we should go this way — here's why."
"We're doing it my way. Decision made."
questions.teach: From most collaborative/deferential (low A) to most assertive/controlling (high A).

questions.q: Order these from MOST reserved to MOST outgoing (Extraversion).
items: Quiet, keeps to themselves, thinks alone
Speaks up when they have something to add
Chatty and warms up quickly in a group
The life of the room, always connecting
questions.teach: Low Extraversion (reserved/introspective) → high Extraversion (gregarious/social).

questions.q: Order these from MOST flexible to MOST formal (Factor D).
items: "I'll bend the process if it helps."
questions.teach: Low Formality (flexible/spontaneous) → high Formality (precise/by-the-book).

questions.q: Order these from MOST driving to MOST steady (Patience).
items: Restless, hates repetition, wants it now
Prefers a brisk pace but can wait
questions.teach: Low Patience (driving/impatient) → high Patience (steady/serene).
